activities_projects.title: Critical Analysis of Rutherford’s Scattering Statistics
activities_projects.objective: To correlate experimental observations with the mathematical probability of alpha-particle deflection to infer nuclear volume.
materials: Detailed diagram of Alpha-particle scattering experiment
steps: Analyze the specific observation that only 1 out of 12,000 particles rebounded at 180 degrees.
Calculate the approximate volume of the nucleus relative to the atom based on these statistical results.
Construct a diagram showing the concentrated positive charge and the vast empty space as proposed by Rutherford's nuclear model.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Synthesizes how rare experimental observations lead to the discovery of the atomic nucleus and the rejection of the Thomson model.
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why the rebound of alpha particles was described as 'firing a 15-inch shell at a piece of tissue paper and it comes back and hits you' (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Stability Paradox: Maxwell’s Theory vs. Rutherford
activities_projects.objective: To investigate the theoretical failure of the nuclear model regarding classical electromagnetic radiation.
materials: Research notes on Maxwell’s electromagnetic theory
Diagram of a spiraling electron path
steps: Examine the classical theory that any accelerated charged particle must radiate energy.
Model the spiral path an electron would theoretically take if it lost energy while revolving around the nucleus.
Discuss how Bohr’s concept of 'discrete orbits' resolved the paradox of atomic collapse by quantizing energy levels.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the necessity of non-radiating discrete orbits in maintaining atomic stability.
activities_projects.evaluation: Provide a technical argument for why Rutherford’s atom should be unstable according to classical physics (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Bohr-Bury Configuration and the Step-wise Rule
activities_projects.objective: To derive the electronic distribution of elements based on energetic stability and the $2n^2$ rule.
materials: Table of electronic configurations of first eighteen elements
steps: Apply the $2n^2$ rule for the first four shells (K, L, M, N).
Analyze the 'Octet Rule' constraint which limits the outermost shell to 8 electrons regardless of shell capacity.
Derive the step-wise filling order for Potassium (Z=19) and Calcium (Z=20) where the N-shell begins before the M-shell is full to maintain stability.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Mastery of advanced electronic configuration and the governing principles of shell capacity.
activities_projects.evaluation: Determine the electronic configuration of an element with atomic number 18 and explain its inert nature (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Valency Trends and Chemical Reactivity
activities_projects.objective: To correlate the number of valence electrons with the periodic nature of element reactivity.
materials: Schematic structures of the first 18 elements
steps: Compare the valence shells of Helium, Neon, and Argon to understand octet stability.
Identify why elements with 1 or 7 valence electrons are highly reactive compared to those with full shells.
Calculate the valency of Phosphorus and Sulphur, explaining why they exhibit multiple combining capacities (e.g., 3 and 5 for Phosphorus).
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that chemical activity is a drive toward a stable octet configuration through sharing or transfer.
activities_projects.evaluation: Differentiate between 'valence electrons' and 'valency' using Magnesium and Nitrogen as examples (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Isotopic Abundance and Average Atomic Mass
activities_projects.objective: To calculate the fractional atomic mass of elements based on the natural distribution of isotopes.
materials: Abundance data for Chlorine and Carbon isotopes
steps: Use the provided data: Cl-35 (75%) and Cl-37 (25%).
Formulate the weighted average equation: $[(35 	imes 75/100) + (37 	imes 25/100)]$.
Research why the mass of a single atom of Chlorine is never 35.5 u, despite the average mass being recorded as such.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Mathematical proficiency in calculating isotopic distributions and understanding the origin of fractional mass units.
activities_projects.evaluation: Calculate the average atomic mass of an element X existing as 90% of isotope 16 and 10% of isotope 18 (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Chadwick’s Discovery and Nucleon Balance
activities_projects.objective: To examine the contribution of the neutron to atomic mass and nuclear stability.
steps: Discuss the 1932 discovery of the neutron and its localization in the nucleus.
Calculate the number of neutrons in the three isotopes of Hydrogen (Protium, Deuterium, Tritium).
Analyze why the mass of an atom is concentrated solely in the nucleus by comparing proton and electron masses.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the definition of mass number (A) as the total count of nucleons (protons + neutrons).
activities_projects.evaluation: An atom has mass number 4 and 2 protons. Determine the number of neutrons and name the element (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Isotopes vs. Isobars: Structural Comparison
activities_projects.objective: To contrast nuclear variants based on atomic identity versus mass similarity.
materials: Data for Calcium (Z=20, A=40) and Argon (Z=18, A=40)
steps: Compare the number of protons and neutrons in the Calcium and Argon nuclei.
Define Isobars as atoms of different elements sharing the same mass number.
Contrast these with isotopes of Carbon regarding their chemical reactivity and electronic configuration.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Distinguishes between elements that share mass versus elements that share identity.
activities_projects.evaluation: Compare isotopes and isobars based on their chemical properties and sub-atomic particle counts (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Industrial and Medical Radiochemistry
activities_projects.objective: To evaluate the specific physical properties of isotopes used in modern technology.
materials: Case studies on Uranium-235, Cobalt-60, and Iodine isotopes
steps: Investigate how the Uranium-235 isotope serves as fuel in nuclear reactors.
Research the role of the Cobalt isotope in radiotherapy and the Iodine isotope in the treatment of goitre.
Identify why isotopes of an element have identical chemical properties despite having different physical properties.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Appreciates the practical application of isotopes in science, energy, and medicine.
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why the chemical properties of isotopes remain identical despite different mass numbers (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Nuclear Notation and Ionic Equilibrium
activities_projects.objective: To master standard notation ${}_{Z}^{A}X$ for neutral atoms and charged ions.
materials: Symbols of elements and common valency table
steps: Practice writing notation for the Oxide ion ($O^{2-}$) and Sodium ion ($Na^{+}$).
Analyze the change in electron count versus the stability of the nucleus during ion formation.
Verify that the mass number remains immutable during chemical ionization processes.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that chemical reactions involve valence electrons, while the nucleus remains unchanged.
activities_projects.evaluation: If an ion $X^{2-}$ has 10 electrons and 8 neutrons, calculate the atomic number and mass number of X (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Evolution of Atomic Models: Comparative Project
activities_projects.objective: To construct a historical timeline and comparative analysis of atomic models.
materials: Summaries of Thomson, Rutherford, and Bohr models
steps: Compare the location and predicted behavior of electrons in the three major models.
Analyze the timeline of the discovery of the three sub-atomic particles.
Summarize how each successive model addressed the specific limitations of its predecessor.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Provides a comprehensive overview of the historical and scientific development of atomic structure.
activities_projects.evaluation: Compare all the proposed models of an atom given in this chapter in a tabular form (10 marks).
